title: Performance, CPI & Amdahl's Law
body:
**The iron law.** Execution time = $IC \times CPI \times T_{clock} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}$, where IC = instruction count, CPI = cycles per instruction, f = clock frequency.

- **CPI from an execution table** = (total clock cycles) / (instructions committed). Speculative instructions that get flushed still consume cycles but don't retire — distinguish *raw* CPI (count all) from *useful* CPI (count only committed).
- **Comparing two machines** ARCH1, ARCH2 on the *same* program: ARCH2 is faster ⟺ $\dfrac{CPI_2}{f_2} < \dfrac{CPI_1}{f_1}$, i.e. $CPI_2 < CPI_1\cdot \dfrac{f_2}{f_1}$.

**Amdahl's law.** If a fraction $f$ of the work is sped up by $s$:
$$Speedup = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{s}}$$
The maximum possible speedup (with $s\to\infty$) is $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$ — the *un-enhanced* part dominates. This is why exploiting ILP everywhere matters: optimizing only part of the code has a hard ceiling.

**AMAT** (memory): $AMAT = t_{hit} + missRate \times missPenalty$ (the hit time is always paid; a miss adds the penalty).

title: Pipeline Hazards: structural / data / control
body:
Classic 5-stage RISC pipeline: **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB**. A *hazard* stops the next instruction from executing in its slot.

**Structural** — two instructions need the same resource the same cycle and it isn't duplicated/pipelined (one memory port for IF+MEM, a non-pipelined unit, too few register write-ports or CDBs).

**Data** — an instruction needs a result not yet produced:
- **RAW** (read-after-write, *true* dependence): the only genuine one — fixed by **forwarding/bypassing**, or a stall (e.g. the load-use hazard needs 1 bubble).
- **WAR** (write-after-read, *anti*) and **WAW** (write-after-write, *output*): *name* dependences, appear only with out-of-order execution, removed by **register renaming**.

**Control** — branches: the next PC is unknown until the branch resolves. Mitigated by **branch prediction**, delay slots, or resolving the branch early (in ID).

Deeper/wider pipelines make every hazard more expensive, motivating forwarding, prediction, and dynamic scheduling.

title: Branch Prediction (static, 1-bit, 2-bit BHT)
body:
Predict a branch's direction/target *before* it resolves so fetch can continue speculatively.

**Static** (fixed at compile time): always-not-taken, always-taken, **BTFNT** (backward-taken / forward-not-taken — matches loops), or compiler/profile hints. Cheap, no adaptation.

**Dynamic — Branch History Table (BHT)** indexed by PC:
- **1-bit**: stores the last outcome. A loop branch mispredicts **twice per episode** — once on the *exit* (predicts taken, it's not-taken) and once on the *first iteration of the next entry* (predicts not-taken, it's taken).
- **2-bit saturating** (strongly/weakly taken/not-taken): a single anomaly only *weakens* the counter without flipping the prediction → **1 misprediction per episode** (the exit). Re-entry predicts taken correctly.

**Exam reasoning.** For *repeatedly re-entered / nested* loops the 2-bit predictor roughly halves loop mispredictions vs 1-bit — its textbook advantage. For a *single long* loop the two are nearly identical (~1 misprediction either way). For *very short* nested loops the 2-bit counter may not warm up, so its worst case can equal the 1-bit best case (this is consistent with theory, not a contradiction).

A **BTB** also supplies the target address; a **return-address stack** predicts returns; **correlating / two-level** predictors use global history.

title: Caches, AMAT & the three C's
body:
**AMAT** = $t_{hit} + missRate \times missPenalty$ (extend per level for multilevel caches).

**The 3 C's of misses:**
- **Compulsory** (cold) — first reference to a block; unavoidable except by prefetch/larger blocks.
- **Capacity** — cache too small to hold the working set.
- **Conflict** — too many blocks map to the same set (direct-mapped suffers most). Fixed by **associativity** or a victim cache.

**Placement:** direct-mapped (1 way) → set-associative (n ways) → fully associative. More associativity = fewer conflict misses, more hit time/energy.

**Write policies:**
- **Write-back** (write updates cache only; memory updated on eviction of a dirty block) vs **write-through** (every write also goes to memory → memory always current).
- **Write-allocate** (a write *miss* fetches the block first) vs **no-write-allocate / write-around** (a write miss writes straight to the next level, the block is not cached).
Typical pairings: write-back + write-allocate, write-through + no-write-allocate (but exams can mix — read the statement!).

*In the MESI exercises the caches are 1-block direct-mapped, so every access to a different block is a conflict miss — that's why miss rates are so high and the fix is more lines / associativity.*

title: Dependences: true vs name (RAW / WAR / WAW)
body:
**True / data dependence (RAW):** instruction j reads a register that instruction i (earlier) writes. The *only* fundamental limit on parallelism — it cannot be removed, only forwarded. To list them: for each source operand of each instruction, find the most recent earlier writer of that register.

**Name dependences** (same register reused, no value flows):
- **Anti (WAR):** j writes a register that i still has to read.
- **Output (WAW):** i and j write the same register.
Both are removed by **register renaming** (Tomasulo / explicit PRF) — that's the whole point of renaming.

**Control dependence:** an instruction's execution depends on a branch outcome (everything after a branch is control-dependent on it).

*Exam tip:* "list all and only the **true** dependences" means RAW only — do **not** list WAR/WAW there. A scoreboard is still constrained by WAR (at WB) and WAW (at issue); Tomasulo is not.

title: Static Scheduling & VLIW
body:
A **VLIW** issues one wide instruction containing several operations, one per functional-unit slot. The **compiler** finds the parallelism and guarantees correctness — the hardware does *no* dynamic hazard detection, so the schedule must already respect every dependence and latency.

**Latency semantics:** an op issued at cycle $C$ produces a result usable by a dependent op at cycle $C + latency$. Schedule a dependent op no earlier than that.

**ASAP / list scheduling:** place each instruction in the earliest cycle where (a) its operands are ready and (b) a suitable unit is free. Respect the **anti-dependence on the index register**: an `ADDUI Ri,Ri,k` that updates the loop index must be scheduled *after* the last memory op that reads the old `Ri` (otherwise the load/store address is corrupted) — which is why the index update and branch usually sit at the bottom of the iteration.

**Optimizations** (only if the question allows): **loop unrolling** (replicate the body to expose more independent ops and amortize loop overhead), **software pipelining** (overlap stages of consecutive iterations).

**FLOPs/CC** = (number of FP ops) / (cycles per iteration). The critical path is the longest dependence chain (often LD → FADD → FADD → SD).

*Filling the grid:* rows = clock cycles, columns = the **specific** units (ALU, MEM1, MEM2, FP-SUM, FP-MUL). Put each op in its issue-cycle row under the exact unit; when several identical units are free, fill the lowest-numbered first. Blank cells are NOPs.

title: Scoreboard (dynamic scheduling, no renaming)
body:
The CDC 6600 **scoreboard** allows out-of-order execution with a *central* control table but **no register renaming**.

**Four stages (columns):**
1. **Issue** — in-order, 1/cycle. **Stalls** on a *structural* hazard (no free FU) or a **WAW** hazard (another in-flight instruction targets the same destination). Resumes the cycle after that prior writer's WB.
2. **Read Operands (RO)** — waits until all source operands are written (**RAW**); $RO \ge ISSUE+1$, and $RO = (\text{producer's WB}) + 1$ if it must wait.
3. **Execute → EXE COMPLETE (EC)** — $EC = RO + latency$.
4. **Write Result (WB)** — $WB = EC + 1$, but **stalls on WAR** (a previous instruction hasn't yet read the register this one overwrites).

**Key limitation:** because there is no renaming, the scoreboard *still suffers WAR and WAW* — exactly the stalls Tomasulo avoids.

*"Is there a configuration that respects this table?"* — infer each FU's latency from $EC-RO$, check structural limits (how many of each unit), and verify the WAW/WAR/RAW stalls are consistent. If a write-after-write was ignored (e.g. a later instruction with the same destination issued before the earlier one's WB), the table is **impossible** → give a corrected one, stating unit count/kind/latency.

title: Tomasulo (reservation stations + renaming)
body:
IBM 360/91 **Tomasulo** adds **reservation stations (RS)** and a **Common Data Bus (CDB)**, giving implicit **register renaming** via RS tags.

**Three stages (columns ISSUE / START EXE / WB):**
1. **Issue** — in-order; **stalls only if there is no free RS** of the needed type. The RS captures ready operand values or the *tags* of producers; the register status points to the RS that will produce each register (this is the renaming).
2. **Start Execute** — when **all operands are available** (read from CDB/regfile) **and a FU is free**; $START \ge ISSUE+1$ and $\ge (\text{producer's WB}) + 1$.
3. **Write Result** — $WB = START + latency$; the result is broadcast on the **CDB** to every waiting RS and the register file. A FU is busy $[START..WB]$; the next user starts at $prevWB+1$. Only **one** result per CDB per cycle (unless there are separate integer/FP CDBs).

**Removes WAR and WAW** — only RAW remains. That's why a load can overwrite a register an earlier, still-pending instruction was using: each gets its own RS tag.

*Versus scoreboard:* distributed control, renaming, result forwarding via CDB → fewer stalls, usually a better CPI on the same code.

title: ROB, Speculation & in-order commit
body:
A **Reorder Buffer (ROB)** lets Tomasulo **speculate past branches** and still give **precise** state: execution is out-of-order but **commit (retire) is in-order**.

**Extra stages/columns:** results live in the ROB (not the register file) until **Commit**. Tables add **Commit**, **ROB idx**, **RSi**, **Unit**.

- **ROB index** = issue order: the 1st issued instruction takes entry 0, the 2nd entry 1, … An entry is **freed at commit**, so with a 10-slot ROB the 11th issued instruction reuses entry 0 *if* entry 0 has already committed (wrap-around).
- **Commit** is in-order, ≥ WB+1 and ≥ previous commit +1; a store writes memory at commit.
- **Misprediction / wrong speculation:** instructions on the wrong path are issued and may execute, but at the branch's resolution they are **flushed** — their ROB entries are discarded and they never commit. The misprediction penalty (e.g. "+10 cycles") is added to the branch's resolution.

**Filling RSi / Unit:** take the lowest-numbered free reservation station *of the matching type* (loads/stores → load RS; FADD → FP RS; integer ops → integer RS) and a free functional unit (LDU1/LDU2, FPU1/FPU2, ALU1); two same-type ops issued close together use different units.

title: Explicit Register Renaming
body:
Instead of renaming *implicitly* through reservation-station tags (Tomasulo), a machine can rename **explicitly** with a large **physical register file (PRF)**.

**Hardware needed (this is extra HW vs a baseline pipeline):**
- a **PRF** with more physical than architectural registers,
- a **rename / map table** translating architectural → physical registers,
- a **free list** of available physical registers (allocate at rename, reclaim at commit),
- recovery state to roll back the map table on a misprediction.

**Effect:** every redefinition of an architectural register is mapped to a **new** physical register, eliminating **WAR and WAW** entirely; only RAW remains.

*Reading a rename table at cycle N:* each architectural register maps to the physical register allocated to its **most recently issued** writer up to cycle N (registers never re-written keep their original mapping). The exact physical numbers depend on the free-list policy — state your assumption.

title: Superscalar & Multithreading
body:
**Multiple issue** — execute >1 instruction per cycle:
- **Superscalar**: hardware decides at run time how many/which instructions to issue (dynamic). Combined with Tomasulo+ROB → out-of-order superscalar.
- **VLIW**: the compiler packs the parallel ops statically (no run-time hazard logic).

**Multithreading** hides stalls by running other threads:
- **Fine-grained**: switch thread **every cycle** (round-robin); hides short stalls, slows single-thread.
- **Coarse-grained (block)**: run one thread **until a costly stall** (e.g. a last-level cache miss), then switch.
- **Simultaneous MT (SMT)**: issue instructions from **multiple threads in the same cycle** on a superscalar back-end — fills issue slots both vertically and horizontally.

These exploit **thread-level parallelism (TLP)** to keep the wide back-end busy when single-thread ILP runs out.

title: Cache Coherence: the MESI protocol
body:
On a shared-memory multiprocessor, a **snooping** coherence protocol keeps caches consistent. **MESI** per-block states:
- **M** (Modified) — this cache's copy is the only valid one and is dirty (memory stale).
- **E** (Exclusive) — only copy, clean (memory current).
- **S** (Shared) — possibly several clean copies.
- **I** (Invalid).

With **write-invalidate**, a write invalidates all other copies. **"Memory up to date for a block" ⟺ no cache holds it in M.**

**Transitions (write-back, write-allocate):**
- **Read miss:** if another cache has it **M** → that cache writes back (memory→current), both become **S**; if others hold **E/S** → all **S**; if no other copy → **E**.
- **Write:** the writer ends in **M**, all others → **I**, memory → stale. A write *miss* first allocates (fetches) the block (write-allocate).

**Write-through variant:** every write also updates memory, so the memory-up-to-date columns are **always yes** and there is no write-back traffic.

**1-block direct-mapped caches** (as in the exam): each cache holds a single block, so accessing a different block **evicts** the current one (write back first only if it was M).

*"Is this pattern possible?"* — check each row against the rules: a **read must bring the block into the reader**; you can never have **two simultaneous owners** (e.g. one E and one M of the same block); a read of an **M** block must trigger a write-back and downgrade to **S**. Any violation ⟹ impossible.

title: Scoreboard vs Tomasulo vs Tomasulo+ROB
body:
| Feature | Scoreboard | Tomasulo | Tomasulo + ROB |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control | centralized | distributed (RS + CDB) | RS + CDB + ROB |
| Register renaming | **no** | yes (RS tags) | yes |
| Removes WAR / WAW | no (stalls) | **yes** | yes |
| Out-of-order execute | yes | yes | yes |
| Result forwarding | via reg file | **CDB broadcast** | CDB broadcast |
| In-order commit | no | no | **yes** |
| Precise exceptions | no | no | **yes** |
| Speculate past branches | no | no | **yes** (flush on mispredict) |

**Take-aways for the exam:**
- Scoreboard *issue* stalls on WAW; *WB* stalls on WAR — these are the trick stalls a "is this table valid?" question probes.
- Tomasulo's renaming lets independent reuses of a register proceed; the only stalls are RAW, no free RS, and CDB arbitration.
- ROB adds **Commit** + **ROB idx** and enables speculation; wrong-path instructions are **flushed** and don't retire (raise the *useful* CPI).
- "Scoreboard and Tomasulo can achieve different performance" is **TRUE** — renaming + CDB remove stalls the scoreboard still pays.

title: How to fill each table (cheat-sheet)
body:
**Scoreboard** (ISSUE / READ OP / EXE COMPL / WB): issue in-order, stall on structural or WAW; $RO$ after operands written (RAW); $EC = RO + lat$; $WB = EC + 1$ (stall on WAR). Infer latency from $EC - RO$.

**Tomasulo** (ISSUE / START / WB): issue in-order, stall only if no free RS; $START$ when operands ready + FU free; $WB = START + lat$; FU busy $[START..WB]$, one result per CDB/cycle.

**Tomasulo + ROB** adds **Commit** (in-order, ≥ WB+1), **ROB idx** (= issue order, freed at commit, wraps), **RSi** (lowest free station of the matching type), **Unit** (LDU1/LDU2, FPU1/FPU2, ALU1; same-type ops take different units). Wrong-path entries are **FLUSHED**.

**VLIW grid** (rows = cycles, columns = *specific* units): op at cycle $C$ → result usable at $C + lat$; put the index update after the last reader of the old index; lowest-numbered free unit first; blanks = NOPs. $FLOPs/CC$ = FP ops / cycles-per-iteration.

**MESI** (per-block state + memory-up-to-date): write ⟹ writer M, others I, memory stale (write-back) / current (write-through); read of an M block ⟹ write-back + downgrade to S; 1-block caches evict on a new block. Memory current ⟺ no holder in M.

**Numbers matter:** commit to the *specific* unit/station/ROB index — `MEM1` not `MU`, `RS5` not `RS`, `S(2)` not `S`.
